statement: Navigation menus must contain no more than seven (plus or minus two) items because that is the limit of human memory.
why_it_spreads: Misapplication of Miller's 1956 paper on short-term memory span to persistent, on-screen navigation.
evidence_against: NN/g (src-nngroup): Miller's limit concerns information held in working memory, not items visible on screen that users can scan and re-read.
Visible menus do not need to be memorised, so the 7 plus/minus 2 constraint does not transfer.
replacement_principle: Size and group navigation around the structure of the content and users' tasks, using clear labels and grouping; legibility and scan-ability matter more than an arbitrary item count.
still_applies_when: Users must hold options in memory (e.g. spoken IVR menus) rather than see them
Very long flat menus still benefit from grouping to reduce scanning effort
